data.title: News Corp Details Progress on US$1 Billion U.S. Share Buy-Back Program
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

An update from News Corporation Shs B Chess Depository Interests repr 1 Sh ( (AU:NWS) ) is now available.

News Corporation has notified the ASX of activity under its previously authorised 2025 Repurchase Program, which allows for the buy-back of up to US$1 billion of its Nasdaq-listed Class A and Class B common stock. The company reported that 141,863,388 Class B shares are on issue in the relevant class and confirmed that no ASX-listed CHESS Depositary Interests will be repurchased under this program, signalling a capital management focus directed primarily at its U.S.-listed equity.

The buy-back will be executed from time to time in the open market or otherwise, subject to market conditions, the company’s share price and other factors. This approach provides management with flexibility to return capital to shareholders and potentially support earnings per share, while leaving the ASX CDI structure unchanged for Australian investors.

The most recent analyst rating on (AU:NWS) stock is a Hold with a A$42.00 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on News Corporation Shs B Chess Depository Interests repr 1 Sh stock, see the AU:NWS Stock Forecast page.

More about News Corporation Shs B Chess Depository Interests repr 1 Sh

News Corporation is a global media and publishing company with a portfolio of newspapers, digital media, and related content businesses. Its equity is listed in multiple markets, including Nasdaq and ASX, with separate Class A and Class B common stock structures serving a broad base of international investors.

data.title: INWIT Cuts 2026 Outlook Amid Contract Clash With TIM–Fastweb JV Plan
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Inwit ( (IT:INW) ) has provided an update.

INWIT’s board has reviewed its strained relationship with anchor tenants TIM and Fastweb, now combined with Vodafone Italia under Swisscom, after they announced plans for a new tower joint venture that INWIT deems incompatible with existing long-term master service agreements. The company rejects claims over alleged contract breaches and early termination, arguing the 2020 agreements underpinned a roughly €10 billion infrastructure transaction that benefited the operators and established INWIT as preferred supplier for new sites.

Amid growing contractual tensions and reduced discretionary investment from tenants, INWIT has cut its 2026 guidance and medium-term outlook, citing a tougher market and uncertainty over future network development. It now expects 2026 revenues of €1.05–1.09 billion, a very high EBITDA margin, solid recurring free cash flow and leverage within a 5x–6x target range, while maintaining at least a €0.55 dividend per share and signaling that any restoration of a constructive relationship with anchor clients could provide upside beyond the current baseline.

The most recent analyst rating on (IT:INW) stock is a Hold with a EUR9.50 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on Inwit stock, see the IT:INW Stock Forecast page.

More about Inwit

Infrastrutture Wireless Italiane, known as INWIT, is an Italian tower company that provides wireless infrastructure for mobile network operators. Its business model relies on long-term, contracted revenue streams from major telecom tenants, positioning it as a key player in supporting mobile network rollout, densification and digital infrastructure across Italy.

Average Trading Volume: 4,109,754

Technical Sentiment Signal: Sell

Current Market Cap: €7.39B

data.title: Starz Entertainment Earnings Call Highlights Streaming Momentum
data.description:
Starz Entertainment Corp ((STRZ)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Starz Entertainment Corp struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, emphasizing record streaming growth, a clean beat on profit targets, and a clear path to positive free cash flow in 2026. Management balanced this optimism with candid acknowledgment of linear TV declines, leverage constraints, and reduced subscriber disclosure, but insisted the business is now on a steadier, more predictable footing.

Record Streaming Gains Anchor Growth Story

Starz said its OTT platform reached an all‑time high of 12.7 million subscribers, up 7.6% versus last year. The company added 370,000 domestic OTT subs in the fourth quarter and lifted total U.S. subscribers by 170,000 to 17.6 million, reinforcing streaming as the core driver of future revenue and scale.

OIBDA Beat Underscores Operational Discipline

The company delivered adjusted OIBDA of $204 million for 2025, topping its $200 million outlook by roughly 2%. Management framed the beat as evidence that tighter cost control, more efficient content spending, and improved monetization are now flowing into earnings, even as the business continues to invest in new shows and markets.

Quarterly Momentum in Profit and Revenue

Fourth‑quarter adjusted OIBDA jumped to $56 million, more than doubling sequentially on the back of lower programming amortization, lower ad and marketing spend, and higher revenue. Quarterly revenue reached $323 million, up 60 basis points from the prior period, signaling improving unit economics even amid a promotional quarter.

Path to Cash Flow and Deleveraging in 2026

Management laid out a detailed road map for 2026, targeting unlevered free cash flow of $80 million to $120 million and positive equity free cash flow for the year. They expect to exit 2026 with leverage of about 2.7x, better than prior guidance and down from 2.9x at year‑end 2025, moving toward a 2.5x long‑term goal.

Building Owned Content for Margin Upside

Starz highlighted progress in shifting toward more owned and co‑owned series to improve future margins and royalty economics. It completed production on its first wholly owned series, Fightland, co‑commissioned with Sky, greenlit Power Origins with an 18‑episode order, and launched the Outlander prequel Blood of My Blood.

Distribution and Bundling Expand Reach

The company restructured its Canadian business, pivoting it into a licensing revenue model to simplify economics and reduce risk. Across markets, management is leaning into bundling and expanded distribution partnerships, arguing that bundles widen the addressable market, add net new subscribers, lift revenue, and improve churn.

Hit Franchises Boost Viewing and Sign‑Ups

Programming performance was another bright spot, with content driving engagement and sub growth. The Spartacus revival drew critical acclaim, while Power Book IV: Force Season 3 delivered a 57% in‑season viewership increase, underscoring how established franchises continue to anchor the service and attract new customers.

Linear Weakness and Promotions Pressure Revenue Mix

Despite streaming gains, linear subscribers continued to decline, weighing on the legacy side of the business. Management also flagged that heavy holiday promotions and more multi‑month low‑churn plans hurt near‑term revenue composition, trading immediate dollars for longer‑term subscriber value.

Investor Unease Over Reduced Subscriber Disclosure

Starting with the March 2026 quarter, Starz will no longer provide subscriber counts, removing a key operating metric many investors track closely. While management argues the business is better assessed on cash flow and profitability, the move could raise questions about transparency and complicate valuation work.

Leverage and Liquidity Remain in Focus

The balance sheet was a central topic, with net debt at $589 million and gross debt at $625 million against just $36 million of cash at year‑end. Starz still has an undrawn $150 million revolver, but management acknowledged leverage as a key constraint and reiterated their commitment to driving it down toward 2.5x over time.

Smoothing Out Volatile Cash and Content Cycles

Executives revisited the company’s history of choppy cash flows and earnings patterns tied to legacy studio‑style content timing. They said that as Starz moves further from that model, 2026 should bring more predictable cash and P&L cadence, though some near‑term synchronization between spend and revenue recognition is still required.

Franchise Dependence and Measured Margin Ambitions

Starz is targeting roughly 20% margins by 2028, but expects only modest improvement in 2026, reflecting continued investment. Management acknowledged that near‑term growth and engagement are heavily concentrated in a few key franchises such as Power and Outlander, which introduces performance risk if any of these brands stumble.

Forward Guidance Signals Gradual but Durable Upside

Looking ahead, the company framed 2026 as an inflection year built on OTT revenue growth and at least $200 million of adjusted OIBDA, with low single‑digit growth from the 2025 base. With cash content spending kept under roughly $650 million and a long‑term plan to convert about 70% of adjusted OIBDA into unlevered free cash flow by 2028, Starz is pitching investors a steady, cash‑centric story.

Starz’s earnings call painted a picture of a streamer leaning into profitable growth, disciplined spending, and gradual deleveraging. While linear erosion, franchise concentration, and lower disclosure keep risk on the radar, record OTT subs and improving cash metrics suggest the platform is maturing, making the stock a potential option for investors seeking measured upside rather than hyper‑growth.

data.title: Intuitive Machines Bets Big on 2026 Turnaround
data.description:
Intuitive Machines, Inc. ((LUNR)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Intuitive Machines’ latest earnings call painted a bold but high‑wire picture, as management leaned into a “transformational” narrative built on acquisitions, a sizable equity raise, and aggressive 2026 growth targets. Executives highlighted improving gross margins and strong program wins, but also acknowledged wider losses, heavy integration costs, and near‑term cash burn that leave execution squarely in the spotlight for investors.

Transformational M&A and Capital Raise

Intuitive Machines closed its Lantaris acquisition in January, following the earlier purchase of Kinetics in October, effectively re‑shaping its scale and capabilities in space communications and data processing. To fund this push, the company completed a $175 million strategic equity raise in February, earmarked for network build‑outs and expanding its Near Space Network Services platform.

Ambitious 2026 Revenue Guidance

Management set an aggressive bar for 2026, projecting revenue between $900 million and $1.0 billion, which they said would be nearly five times the 2025 level. Leadership stressed that this surge is not purely aspirational, noting that approximately two‑thirds of the 2026 revenue target is already anchored by contracted backlog.

Significant Backlog Growth and Visibility

On a combined basis, Intuitive Machines estimated backlog at roughly $943 million as of February month‑end, incorporating its recent Space Development Agency Tranche 3 award. This expanding book of business, spanning multiple years, gives investors improved visibility into future revenue streams as programs move from award to execution.

Q4 Gross Margin Improvement

Fourth‑quarter 2025 gross profit reached $8.5 million, translating to a 19% gross margin and marking a key step toward better economics. Management credited the uplift to a growing mix of higher‑margin services such as NSNS and cost reductions achieved on fixed‑price contracts that had previously pressured profitability.

Operational Milestones and Launch Progress

Beyond the financials, the company emphasized recent technical execution, including its second lunar mission and the successful launch of EchoStar 25, which is now undergoing on‑orbit checks. NASA’s Power & Propulsion Element was successfully powered up, and the IM‑3 mission, including a lunar data relay satellite, is advancing through testing and integration.

National Security Awards and Customer Diversification

Intuitive Machines underscored its expanding role in national security space after winning the SDA Tranche 3 Tracking Layer award, on top of its involvement in earlier tranches and related programs. Management expects a balanced revenue mix going forward, with roughly 40% from commercial customers, 40% from civil agencies, and 20% from national security work, reducing dependence on any single segment.

Service Strategy and Recurring Revenue Potential

A central pillar of the long‑term story is the company’s service‑oriented model, anchored by NSNS and long‑duration infrastructure missions. Management referenced a multi‑billion‑dollar NSNS contract scope and highlighted the first of five planned lunar data relay satellites, which are intended to support subscription‑like connectivity and higher‑margin, recurring service revenue over time.

Cash Position and Liquidity Management

The company ended 2025 with $583 million in cash before closing Lantaris, providing a buffer for integration and growth investments. After deploying cash for the acquisition and raising new equity, Intuitive Machines reported about $272 million in cash as of February and said it believes this is sufficient to fund operations and strategic initiatives.

Year‑Over‑Year Operational Cash Improvement

While still firmly in investment mode, the company pointed to progress on cash efficiency, with annual free cash flow improving by $11.7 million compared with 2024. Operating cash usage declined by $43.3 million year over year even as capital expenditures increased, suggesting early benefits from scaling and cost discipline.

Persistent Profitability Pressures

Despite margin gains, earnings remained under heavy pressure, with the Q4 operating loss widening to $33.1 million versus $13.4 million a year earlier. Adjusted EBITDA was negative $19.1 million in the quarter, deepening from the prior year’s loss, and management acknowledged that the business will remain loss‑making near term even as it targets improvement by 2026.

Acquisition‑Related Costs Weigh on Results

Quarterly SG&A expenses surged to $40.2 million, including $10.8 million in transaction costs related to the Lantaris deal that materially inflated the quarter’s loss. Integration spending is also expected to remain elevated in the short run, as the company combines operations and seeks synergies across its enlarged portfolio.

Liquidity Hit from Lantaris Purchase

The Lantaris transaction required roughly $430 million of cash outflows, a substantial deployment relative to the year‑end balance. Even after the February equity raise, this acquisition sharply reduced reported cash, leaving less cushion and heightening the importance of executing against backlog and managing capital spending tightly.

Backlog Timing and Government Delays

Standalone year‑end 2025 backlog slipped to $213.1 million from $235.9 million, a nearly 10% decline that management mainly tied to U.S. government shutdowns and appropriations delays. Those disruptions also created revenue headwinds in Q4, underscoring how dependent certain programs remain on government budgeting cycles and procurement timing.

Negative Free Cash Flow and CapEx Commitments

Free cash flow remained negative at $22.9 million in Q4 and $56 million for the full year, reflecting the ongoing build‑out of NSNS and broader infrastructure. Management argued that elevated CapEx, including $15.6 million in the fourth quarter, is necessary to underpin future high‑margin service revenue, but it extends the period before self‑funded growth is achieved.

Incomplete View of the Combined Company

Investors are still waiting for a full picture of the enlarged enterprise because 2025 results do not include Lantaris and pro forma financials are still being finalized. This lag creates some near‑term uncertainty around the true earnings power and synergy potential of the combined platform ahead of the ramp envisioned for 2026.

Exposure to Government Award Timing

Several important growth catalysts, including the Lunar Terrain Vehicle and other lunar and exploration programs, remain tied to government procurement timelines that have already shown vulnerability to delays. Management flagged that reforms and budget processes can shift award schedules, introducing execution and revenue‑recognition risk despite robust long‑term demand signals.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, Intuitive Machines is steering investors toward a 2026 inflection, with revenue guidance of $900 million to $1.0 billion backed by roughly two‑thirds contracted backlog and a total combined backlog near $943 million. The company is targeting continued gross margin expansion and a return to positive adjusted EBITDA next year, while working through negative free cash flow and integrating Lantaris within a tighter but, in management’s view, adequate liquidity position.

The earnings call showcased a company aiming to leap from early‑stage operator to scaled space infrastructure player, powered by acquisitions, major awards, and a push into recurring services. For shareholders, the opportunity is clear but execution risk is front‑and‑center, as Intuitive Machines must translate its rich backlog and heavy investments into profitable, sustainable growth by 2026 and beyond.
